text: The surgeon begins dissecting the area of the hernia sac during a robotic transabdominal retromuscular repair, focusing on the mid portion of the procedure.

The surgeon has completed the dissection, revealing a large peritoneal gap with no hernia sac present. The ipsilateral posterior rectal sheath has been dissected off, and the surgeon is entering the contralateral retroactive space. The visual field shows the completed dissection area and the peritoneal gap.

The surgeon begins cutting through the posterior rectus sheath, initially cutting only the posterior lamella of the internal oblique. After shimmying the scissors to find the correct plane, the surgeon successfully cuts through both the posterior lamella of the internal oblique and the transversus abdominis, performing a transversus abdominis release (TAR). This technique releases the posterior rectus sheath from its attachments at the linea semilunaris, allowing for medialization and closure of the central gap without significant tension.

The surgeon navigates the preperitoneal space, carefully separating the peritoneum from the transversalis fascia. In the upper abdomen, the surgeon enters the pre-transversalis fascia plane, revealing the space between the transversalis fascia and the transverse abdominis.

The surgeon completes the release of the posterior rectus sheath, achieving closure of the central defect without significant tension. The final product shows the repaired area with the defect closed.
